items.hook: Tattoo-friendly onsens in Kusatsu
items.name: Kusatsu Onsen — Tattoo-Friendly Public Baths
items.why: Three public onsens in Kusatsu — Sai-no-kawara, Otaki-no-yu, and Goza-no-yu — welcome tattoos of any size, making this one of Japan's most accessible hot-spring towns for inked travellers.

items.hook: Seven inked-friendly baths near Kyoto
items.name: Kinosaki Onsen
items.why: Charming historic onsen town near Kyoto where all seven public bathhouses permit tattoos of any size — perfect for a ryokan-and-yukata overnight escape.
items.location_text: Kinosaki Onsen, Toyooka, Hyogo Prefecture, Japan

items.hook: Osaka's top restaurant, no sponsors
items.name: Sandaime Wakihiko Shouten
items.why: One of the creator's genuine top-3 restaurants in Osaka — a local favourite in Fukushima Ward worth seeking out for an authentic, unpaid recommendation.
items.location_text: 2 Chome-10-22 Fukushima, Fukushima Ward, Osaka 553-0003, Japan

items.hook: A forest of trees from one trunk
items.name: Daisugi — Northern Kyoto
items.why: Ancient Japanese forestry technique creating groves where dozens of straight cedar trunks grow from a single base — most prevalent in Kyoto's northern districts and visually unlike anything else.

items.hook: A 650-year-old imperial cherry tree
items.name: Kokonoe Sakura
items.why: Said to have been planted by an emperor 650 years ago, this ancient cherry tree now blooms on just one remaining branch once a year — a fleeting, almost mythic floral event.
items.date_info: Single annual bloom, typically late March–April

items.hook: Japan's widest pine spans 50 metres
items.name: Yuryu Pine Tree
items.why: One of Japan's greatest pine trees — only 2 metres tall yet spreading over 50 metres wide, a jaw-dropping natural sculpture recognised as a national treasure.
items.location_text: Yuryu Pine Tree, Kyoto Prefecture, Japan

items.hook: Fishermen's floating houses in Ine
items.name: Funaya Boat Houses — Ine
items.why: Ine's iconic funaya are traditional wooden boathouses built directly over the sea, where the ground floor is a garage for fishing boats and the upper floor is a home — virtually unchanged for centuries.

items.hook: Secret mountain onsen near Kobe
items.name: Private Onsen in the Mountains — Kobe
items.why: A hidden private hot-spring bath tucked into the mountains outside Kobe, offering complete seclusion and stunning natural scenery — the kind of off-grid retreat rarely featured in guidebooks.
items.location_text: Mountains near Kobe, Hyogo Prefecture, Japan

items.hook: A mossy hidden temple in Ohara
items.name: Kochidani Amida-ji Temple
items.why: A barely-visited hillside temple in Ohara, north of Kyoto, draped in moss and surrounded by dense cedar forest — a quintessential wabi-sabi off-the-beaten-path discovery.
items.location_text: Kochidani Amida-ji Temple, Ohara, Kyoto Prefecture, Japan

items.hook: Sacred sunrise hike above Kyoto
items.name: Mt. Atago
items.why: Kyoto's highest peak at 924 m, home to the revered Atago Shrine, with a pre-dawn hike rewarded by sweeping views over the city and Arashiyama forest below.

items.hook: Ancient village life in rural Tamba
items.name: Tamba, Hyogo
items.why: A rural mountainous area of Hyogo Prefecture dotted with traditional farmhouses, ancient shrines, and autumn foliage — a low-key escape from Osaka and Kyoto that few tourists reach.

items.hook: Shinto shrine hidden in Kyoto forest
items.name: Kamo Jinja Shrine — Ukyo Ward
items.why: A quiet Shinto shrine in Kyoto's Ukyo Ward surrounded by ancient trees, offering a meditative escape well off the standard tourist trail through the city's western forests.
items.location_text: Kamo Jinja Shrine, Ukyo Ward, Kyoto, Japan

items.hook: Wild monkeys soak in hot springs
items.name: Snow Monkey Park
items.why: Watch Japanese macaques bathe in steaming outdoor hot springs against a snowy mountain backdrop — one of Japan's most surreal and iconic wildlife encounters.
items.location_text: Jigokudani Monkey Park, Yamanouchi, Nagano, Japan
items.date_info: Best in winter (December-March) when snow is present

items.hook: Italian-Japanese fusion on Kyoto's oldest canal
items.name: Arancino Kyoto
items.why: Multi-course Italian-Japanese fusion beside the historic Takase River, where every dish is as visually stunning as it is flavourful — you'll photograph every course.

items.hook: Japan's ultimate comfort meal under $10
items.name: Teishokuya (Japanese set meal restaurants)
items.why: Teishokuya diners serve a balanced set meal — rice, miso soup, protein, and sides — on every street corner across Japan. Search 'Teishokuya' on Google Maps to find one near you.

items.hook: A hall that floats above a sacred lake
items.name: Ukimido Floating Hall
items.why: A serene wooden pavilion that appears to hover on the surface of Lake Biwa, offering an impossibly tranquil scene straight from a classical Japanese painting.
items.location_text: Ukimido Floating Hall, Lake Biwa, Shiga, Japan

items.hook: A hidden temple swallowed by forest
items.name: Ryuzoji Temple
items.why: Hidden deep in a mountain forest in Hyogo, this hard-to-find temple rewards determined explorers with moss-covered stone and near-total solitude.

items.hook: Squeeze through rocks to test your heart
items.name: Aga Shrine
items.why: Legend says only those with a pure heart can pass through the narrow rock crevice at the entrance — a quirky, mythic ritual that makes this Shiga shrine unforgettable.
items.date_info: Cherry blossom season (late March–April) for sakura viewing

items.hook: Pink clouds before cherry blossom season
items.name: Jonangu Shrine (Plum Blossoms)
items.why: Jonangu's garden erupts in pink-and-white plum blossoms in mid-February — weeks before cherry season — with far smaller crowds. Arrive early or just before closing.
items.date_info: Mid-to-late February; arrive early or 30 minutes before closing to avoid crowds

items.hook: Plum blossoms framing ancient bamboo statues
items.name: Hogon-in (Plum Blossoms at Arashiyama)
items.why: The stone statues in front of Hogon-in temple frame beautifully against blooming Japanese plum trees in February, a lesser-visited alternative to Arashiyama's crowds.
items.date_info: Mid-to-late February for plum blossom peak

items.hook: Hidden sakura spots locals never share
items.name: Rural Japan Hidden Sakura Hunting
items.why: Rent a car and drive through small towns and villages to find breathtaking, crowd-free cherry blossom spots that never appear on Google Maps.
items.date_info: Cherry blossom season, typically late March–April

items.hook: Fill your bottle with natural hot spring
items.name: Onsen Water Stations (温泉スタンド)
items.why: Scattered across Japan, these roadside dispensers let you take home real geothermal onsen water. Search '温泉スタンド' on Google Maps to find the nearest one.

items.hook: Sip the world's finest matcha in Uji
items.name: Uji
items.why: A quiet town just south of Kyoto renowned as Japan's premier matcha-producing region, with historic tea houses, temples, and riverside scenery.

items.hook: Japan's coastline begins at Kyoto's shore
items.name: Kyoto by the Sea (Kyotango/Kinosaki Coast)
items.why: Kyoto Prefecture's northern coast along the Sea of Japan offers dramatic cliffs, sandy beaches, and fresh seafood far from the usual tourist trail.

items.hook: Thatched rooftops frozen in feudal Japan
items.name: Kayabuki no Sato
items.why: A perfectly preserved village of traditional thatched-roof farmhouses in northern Kyoto, often shrouded in morning mist — a surreal step back in time.
items.location_text: Kayabuki no Sato, Miyama, Nantan, Kyoto Prefecture, Japan

items.hook: Ancient shrine route above Lake Biwa
items.name: Mount Hiei & Omihachiman (Shiga Prefecture)
items.why: Shiga Prefecture hides Mount Hiei's sacred ridge trail and the canal-laced merchant town of Omihachiman — all within an hour of Kyoto yet completely tourist-free.

items.hook: Rural Nagano is Japan's best-kept secret
items.name: Rural Life in North Nagano Prefecture
items.why: Northern Nagano offers world-class ski resorts, Sea of Japan beaches, weekly hot springs, Lord-of-the-Rings hiking trails, and farm-fresh food — all at 1.5–2x less cost than Kyoto.
items.price_text: Rent from ~$570/month; food costs 1.5–2x cheaper than Kyoto

items.hook: Ten days through Hokkaido's wild heart
items.name: Hokkaido Summer Road Trip: Sapporo to Sapporo Loop
items.why: A 10–14 day self-drive loop from Sapporo hits active volcanoes, caldera lakes, lavender fields, wild waterfalls, and coastal hot springs largely free of foreign tourists.
items.date_info: Best in summer (June–August)

items.hook: Two giant sea stacks guard a secret beach
items.name: Ebisu Rock & Daikoku Rock
items.why: Dramatic volcanic sea stacks rising from Noboribetsu's coastline, best accessed by car, with barely another visitor in sight.

items.hook: Hell Valley bubbles with sulphur and steam
items.name: Noboribetsu Jigokudani (Hell Valley)
items.why: A volcanic crater valley spewing boiling sulphurous springs with vivid ochre earth — the geological source of Noboribetsu's famous onsen resorts.

items.hook: Japan's clearest caldera lake, peacefully alone
items.name: Lake Shikotsu
items.why: One of Japan's deepest and clearest volcanic caldera lakes, ringed by forested peaks — far less crowded than Lake Toya but just as stunning.

items.hook: Purple lavender fields rolled across the valley
items.name: Furano
items.why: Hokkaido's iconic lavender capital erupts in purple and multicoloured flower fields each July, framed by rolling hills and snow-capped peaks.
items.date_info: Peak lavender mid-July

items.hook: A bamboo festival in a secret onsen village
items.name: Takenoko Matsuri (Bamboo Festival), Nozawa Onsen
items.why: Each summer this charming onsen village fills with food stalls serving bamboo shoot dishes and traditional dance performances — a rarely photographed local festival.
items.date_info: Summer (typically June–July)

items.hook: Cedar giants guard a mythic mountain shrine
items.name: Togakushi Shrine Okusha (戸隠神社 奥社)
items.why: A 30–40 minute forest trail lined with towering 400-year-old cedar trees leads to a legendary shrine in the mountains — one of Japan's most beautiful spiritual paths.
items.location_text: Togakushi Shrine Okusha, Togakushi, Nagano City, Nagano Prefecture, Japan
items.date_info: Especially recommended in summer to escape heat

items.hook: A wild outdoor hot spring on a volcano's flank
items.name: Tsubame Onsen
items.why: A secluded outdoor onsen perched on the slopes of Mount Myoko, offering a genuine wild bathing experience far from tourist-resort crowds.
items.location_text: Tsubame Onsen, Mt. Myoko, Myoko City, Niigata Prefecture, Japan

items.hook: A 600-year-old dragon pine still alive
items.name: Yuryu-no-Matsu (Dragon Pine), Yoshiminedera Temple
items.why: A legendary 600-year-old pine tree trained into a sweeping dragon-like canopy at a hillside temple in western Kyoto — one of Japan's three great pine trees.
items.location_text: Yoshiminedera Temple, Oharano Oshio-cho, Nishikyo-ku, Kyoto, Japan

items.hook: A Ghibli village frozen in time
items.name: Kayabuki No Sato
items.why: A charming thatched-roof village in northern Kyoto Prefecture surrounded by forests, mountains, and rivers — with far fewer tourists than central Kyoto.
items.location_text: Kayabuki No Sato, Miyama, Nantan, Kyoto Prefecture, Japan
items.date_info: Special illumination events in May and December

items.hook: A temple hiding deep in forest
items.name: Kochidani Amida-ji Temple
items.why: A hidden forest temple that looks abandoned at first glance — closed January–February but breathtaking when open, with virtually zero tourist crowds.
items.location_text: Kochidani Amida-ji Temple (阿弥陀寺), Kyoto Prefecture, Japan
items.date_info: Closed to visitors in January and February

items.hook: Draw your fortune at a river shrine
items.name: Kifune Shrine
items.why: A mystical Shinto shrine in the Kyoto mountains where you can try Omikuji fortune-telling — draw a slip from the water to reveal your fate.
items.location_text: Kifune Shrine, Kibune, Sakyo Ward, Kyoto, Japan

items.hook: A completely empty ancient temple
items.name: Kuhonji Temple
items.why: A tranquil, tourist-free Buddhist temple in rural Nantan City — stumbled upon by accident and utterly serene with no crowds whatsoever.
items.location_text: Kuhonji Temple (九品寺), Nantan, Kyoto Prefecture, Japan

items.hook: Sushi aged in red vinegar for a decade
items.name: Sushi Akazu
items.why: Osaka's standout omakase spot specializing in sushi seasoned with 10-year-aged red vinegar — a rare 20-course tasting menu that demands a reservation.

items.hook: Four unmissable summer festivals in Japan
items.name: Japan Summer Festival Guide
items.why: Four standout seasonal festivals: Nara Tokae lanterns (Aug 5–14), Daimonji fire mountains in Kyoto (Aug 16), Himeji Yukata Festival (Jun 24–25), and Matsuo Taisha Hassaku Festival (Sep 3).
items.date_info: Nara Tokae: Aug 5–14; Daimonji (Kyoto): Aug 16; Yukata Festival (Himeji): Jun 24–25; Hassaku Festival (Matsuo Taisha, Kyoto): Sep 3

items.hook: Princess Mononoke vibes along the river
items.name: Kinosaki to Hozukyo River Hike
items.why: A lush riverside hike from Kinosaki bus stop to Hozukyo JR station through dramatic forest gorges that feel straight out of a Studio Ghibli film.
items.location_text: Kinosaki bus stop to Hozukyo Station, Hozu River, Kyoto Prefecture, Japan
items.date_info: Best in spring and autumn

items.hook: Kyoto's mountaintop shrine secret
items.name: Atago Shrine (Atago-san)
items.why: A mysterious, atmospheric shrine perched atop Atago Mountain in Kyoto — relatively undiscovered and perfect for fresh mountain air and spiritual calm.

items.hook: Kobe's cozy dandan noodle den
items.name: Enishi Dandan Noodles (enishi_dandannoodles)
items.why: A small, intimate noodle shop in Kobe serving exceptional dandan noodles — the kind of snug, soul-warming spot that feels straight out of Spirited Away.

items.hook: Japan's sandbar to the sky
items.name: Amanohashidate
items.why: One of Japan's Three Views — a 3.6 km pine-covered sandbar stretching across Miyazu Bay, best admired upside-down between your legs for a classic floating-bridge illusion.

items.hook: Fishermen's village frozen in time
items.name: Ine
items.why: A remote fishing village on the Sea of Japan where traditional funaya boathouses sit directly on the water — one of Japan's most scenic and least-visited coastal spots.

items.hook: Bamboo grove in every season
items.name: Arashiyama
items.why: Kyoto's iconic riverside district transforms dramatically between summer green and winter snow — the bamboo grove, Tenryu-ji garden, and mountain backdrop are stunning year-round.

items.hook: A thousand stone monks in hiding
items.name: Otagi Nenbutsu-ji Temple
items.why: A remote Kyoto temple packed with 1,200 moss-covered stone rakan statues, each with a unique expression — every visit reveals a new face you've never noticed before.
items.location_text: Otagi Nenbutsu-ji, 2-5 Saganisonin Monzen-cho, Ukyo-ku, Kyoto

items.hook: Collect a deity's inked blessing
items.name: Goshuin (temple stamp collecting)
items.why: Getting a handwritten Goshuin stamp at temples and shrines is a meaningful ritual and the most unique souvenir from Japan — costs 300–500 yen per stamp and requires a special Goshuincho book.

items.hook: Kyoto's oldest antique flea market
items.name: Kitano Tenmangu Shrine Flea Market (Tenjin-san)
items.why: A beloved monthly antique and food market held on the 25th of every month at Kitano Tenmangu Shrine — an unmissable slice of local Kyoto life with vendors selling ceramics, vintage kimono, and street food.
items.location_text: Kitano Tenmangu Shrine, Bakuro-cho, Kamigyo-ku, Kyoto
items.date_info: 25th of every month, 08:00–16:00

items.hook: Sprawling temple market at dawn
items.name: Toji Temple Flea Market (Kobo-san)
items.why: Kyoto's largest flea market wraps around the iconic five-story pagoda of Toji Temple on the 21st of each month (plus first Sundays) — arrive early for the best antique finds and street food.
items.date_info: 21st of every month and first Sunday of each month, 08:00–16:00

items.hook: Floral mochi in a century-old house
items.name: Rokujuan (麓寿庵)
items.why: A Kyoto cafe inside a 100-year-old townhouse serving exquisite Warabi Mochi decorated with real flowers — a beautiful, rare sweet worth seeking out.

items.hook: Nail the onsen etiquette perfectly
items.name: Japan Onsen Etiquette Rules
items.why: Five essential onsen rules — no phones, tie hair up, keep your towel dry, check tattoo policies, and rinse after the sauna — so you never accidentally offend.

items.hook: An $18 unagi set lunch in Tokyo
items.name: Unagi no Naruse
items.why: A beloved eel-rice chain packed with Tokyo office workers at lunch — the full unagi set costs under $18, making it one of the city's great value splurges.
items.location_text: 1-8-2 Higashi-Shinbashi, Minato-ku, Tokyo, Caretta Shiodome B1
items.price_text: Under $18 for a full unagi set

items.hook: A $70 Kaiseki that outshines $250 ones
items.name: Kintarou Onsen Hotel Kaiseki Dinner
items.why: A stunning multi-course seasonal Kaiseki dinner at a Toyama onsen hotel — just $70 yet ranked more memorable than far pricier meals. Peak Japanese seasonal cooking at a real-deal price.

items.hook: A castle glowing in winter darkness
items.name: Matsumoto Castle Winter Light-Up
items.why: Japan's iconic black castle is illuminated nightly through winter, reflecting on its moat in an otherworldly glow — a free annual spectacle in Matsumoto city.
items.date_info: December 14 to February 16, 18:00–22:00 nightly (annual event, confirm each year)

items.hook: The temple where wishes get eyes
items.name: Shorinzan Daruma-ji Temple
items.why: Japan's most famous Daruma doll temple, where visitors paint one eye on a wish-doll and complete it when the goal is achieved — a living ritual of perseverance and intention.
items.location_text: Shorinzan Daruma-ji Temple, Takasaki, Gunma, Japan

items.hook: Coffee shop with lucky charm vibes
items.name: Twisted Brew
items.why: A characterful local coffee shop near Daruma-ji Temple that pairs perfectly with a visit to the landmark — quirky name, local atmosphere, great stop on the Takasaki Daruma trail.

items.hook: Lunch spot beside a wish temple
items.name: Sunny Kitchen
items.why: A friendly local restaurant close to Shorinzan Daruma-ji Temple — ideal for a meal before or after making your Daruma wish in Takasaki.

items.hook: Hot springs that heat a Nagano winter
items.name: Jizo Onsen
items.why: A local onsen in Ueda, Nagano favored by residents braving the harsh mountain winters — authentic, no-frills, and exactly the kind of warming soak that makes cold-weather Japan trips unforgettable.
items.date_info: Best appreciated in winter months

items.hook: Nagano is more than just ski slopes
items.name: Nagano Prefecture Winter Guide
items.why: Beyond world-class skiing, Nagano offers preserved old towns (Matsumoto, Obuse, Ueda), cozy onsen towns, and Japan's richest miso ramen — best experienced in the full winter season.
items.date_info: Winter season (December–March)

items.hook: Old streets frozen in feudal time
items.name: Matsumoto
items.why: One of Nagano's most beautifully preserved historic cities, home to Japan's oldest original castle and charming old-town streets that look like a winter dream under snow.
items.date_info: Stunning in winter

items.hook: Miso ramen that redefines the bowl
items.name: Nagano Miso Ramen
items.why: Nagano's regional miso ramen is said to be the richest and most flavorful in Japan — a must-eat after a cold day on the slopes or wandering winter streets.

items.hook: Kyoto's hidden-sign shopping labyrinth
items.name: Kyoto Hidden Shops
items.why: Kyoto's backstreets are lined with tiny unnamed shops — handwritten kanji boards, quiet doorways hiding handmade ceramics, wagashi, and curiosities you won't find anywhere else in Japan.

items.hook: Sakura that blooms for weeks not days
items.name: Nagano Sakura Season
items.why: Nagano's mountainous terrain staggers cherry blossom blooms across weeks — some valleys peaking while mountains are still bare, letting you chase sakura longer than almost anywhere else in Japan.
items.date_info: Late March through early May depending on elevation

items.hook: Skip cherry blossom season crowds
items.name: Japan Cherry Blossom Season: What to Know
items.why: Cherry blossom season is Japan's most unpredictable, crowded, and expensive time to visit. Arrive too early and the trees are bare; unless sakura is your sole goal, consider shoulder seasons instead.
items.date_info: Cherry blossom season typically late March to mid-April

items.hook: Jelly noodles made from seaweed
items.name: Tokoroten (Seaweed Jelly Noodles) in Joetsu
items.why: Tokoroten is a traditional Japanese jelly noodle made from seaweed, served sweet or sour with multiple dipping sauces and mustard. A uniquely textured local specialty best discovered in Joetsu.

itinerary.creator: empty.japan
itinerary.title: Kyoto Off the Map
itinerary.summary: Three days roaming the forests, hillside temples, and hidden markets that tourists miss entirely. No crowds, no queues — just the real Kyoto.
itinerary.best_for: Slow travellers who want nature, spirituality, and genuine discovery over highlight-reel ticking.

days.theme: Hidden temples deep in Arashiyama's back lanes

stops.title: Arashiyama Bamboo Grove + Tenryu-ji
stops.what: Walk the bamboo grove, explore the Tenryu-ji garden.
stops.why: Stunning year-round; beautiful early before tour groups arrive.
stops.tip: Get your Goshuincho stamped at Tenryu-ji (300–500 yen) — first of many on the trip.

stops.title: Otagi Nenbutsu-ji Temple
stops.why: Each face is unique — you keep spotting new ones.
stops.transit_next: 20-min walk or 10-min taxi back toward Arashiyama

stops.title: Lunch in Arashiyama village
stops.what: Pick a riverside tofu or soba spot along Nakanoshima.
stops.why: Refuel before the afternoon; great views over the Oi River.

stops.title: Arancino Kyoto
stops.what: Multi-course Italian-Japanese fusion beside Takase River.
stops.why: Every course is as photogenic as it is delicious.

days.pace_note: Compact western Kyoto loop; easy walking between stops.

days.theme: Northern wilds — Kibune valley and Daisugi groves

stops.title: Kifune Shrine
stops.what: Try water Omikuji fortune-telling at the river shrine.
stops.why: Atmospheric mountain setting; ritual you won't find elsewhere.
stops.transit_next: Short walk into the surrounding cedar valley
stops.tip: Take the Eizan Railway to Kibune-guchi (35 min from Demachiyanagi) — no need to rent a car.

stops.title: Daisugi Cedar Groves
stops.what: See dozens of straight trunks rising from a single base.
stops.why: An ancient forestry technique that looks like nothing else.

stops.title: Kochidani Amida-ji Temple
stops.what: Explore a barely-visited mossy hillside temple in Ohara.
stops.why: Pure wabi-sabi — cedar, moss, total silence.
stops.transit_next: 10-min walk to Ohara village centre for dinner

stops.title: Ohara village dinner
stops.what: Simple set meal at a local shokudo in Ohara.
stops.why: Small village restaurants close early — eat here before heading back.

days.pace_note: One valley per half-day; bus connections keep it relaxed.

days.theme: Western hills ancient pines + flea market morning

stops.title: Kitano Tenmangu Flea Market (Tenjin-san)
stops.what: Browse ceramics, vintage kimono, and street food stalls.
stops.why: Beloved monthly market — a real slice of local Kyoto life.
stops.transit_next: 30-min taxi or bus west to Nishiyama
stops.tip: Market runs on the 25th of every month, 08:00–16:00 — plan your dates around it.

stops.title: Dragon Pine at Yoshiminedera Temple
stops.what: See a 600-year-old pine trained into a sweeping dragon canopy.
stops.why: One of Japan's three great pines — jaw-dropping natural sculpture.

stops.title: Rokujuan Cafe
stops.what: Warabi Mochi decorated with real flowers in a century-old townhouse.
stops.why: A rare, beautiful sweet worth the hunt.

stops.title: Farewell dinner at Arancino Kyoto
stops.what: Return to the Takase River canal for a final long dinner.
stops.why: Perfect close — every course a photograph, every bite a memory.

days.pace_note: Easy pace — market, one temple, one cafe, one dinner.

logistics.getting_around: Eizan Railway for the northern valley (Kibune); local buses and taxis for Ohara and Nishiyama. No car needed within the city.
logistics.where_to_stay: Stay in Nakagyo or Higashiyama — central enough for all three day-trip directions.
logistics.best_time: Year-round. Plum blossom (Feb) and cherry season (late Mar–Apr) add spectacular colour to most stops.
logistics.budget: Budget ~3,000–5,000 yen/day for entry fees and transport. Pick up a Goshuincho book (500–1,000 yen) for temple stamps at 300–500 yen each.
